steps.text: Decontaminate cages.
Document the animal facility design parameters and operational procedures.
Test the facility to verify that the design and operational parameters have been met.
Re-test facilities annually or after significant modification to ensure operational parameters are maintained.
Modify verification criteria, as necessary, by operational experience.
Provide appropriate communication systems between the animal facility and the outside.
Develop and implement provisions for emergency communication and emergency access or egress.

preconditions.text: Prior to removal from the cabinet

constraints.text: The cage wash area is designed to accommodate the use of high-pressure spray systems, humidity, strong chemical disinfectants, and 180°F water temperatures during the cage/equipment cleaning process.

Autoclave doors are interlocked so that only one can be opened at any time and are automatically controlled so that the outside door to the autoclave can only be opened after the decontamination cycle has been completed.
Gas discharge from the autoclave chamber is HEPA-filtered or decontaminated.

steps.text: Counsel staff who refuse recommended immunization against a vaccine-preventable disease.
Document the staff members’ lack of protection in the medical record.
Offer staff the chance to seek medical advice when changes in health status occur.
Direct staff with specific concerns (such as working with biohazards while immunocompromised or reproductive capacity effects) to seek confidential medical counseling with a qualified clinician.

preconditions.text: Staff refuses recommended immunization against a vaccine-preventable disease
Changes in staff health status occur
Staff has specific concerns, such as working with biohazards while immunocompromised or reproductive capacity effects

constraints.text: Acceptance of vaccinations that are medically indicated should not be a precondition of employment in biomedical research laboratories (with few exceptions).
Do not require periodic medical evaluations solely because staff work with biological hazards, unless justified by a substantial risk of exposure to biohazards.
The major sources of laboratory-associated psittacosis are contact with and exposure to infectious aerosols in the handling, care, or the necropsy of naturally or experimentally infected birds.
Occupational exposures that can lead to infection most often occur through exposure to mucosal tissues in the eyes, nose, and respiratory tract.

preconditions.text: Importation of this agent requires CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
Domestic transport of this agent may require a permit from USDA APHIS VS.
A DoC permit may be required for the export of this agent to another country.

constraints.text: Laboratory-associated coccidioidomycosis is a documented hazard of working with sporulating cultures of Coccidioides spp.

steps.text: Destroy non-essential poliovirus materials.
Contain retained poliovirus material in certified poliovirus-essential facilities.
Implement risk mitigation measures for OPV2 potentially infectious materials per the Guidance for non-poliovirus facilities.
Destroy all PV1 and OPV3 materials not essential for research or other work.
Apply additional GAPIII physical laboratory containment measures for WPV and VDPV materials.
Apply GAPIII containment to types 1 and OPV3.

preconditions.text: Final eradication of all poliovirus types is achieved

constraints.text: Containment measures must include a biorisk management system, biosafety, security, and physical laboratory features.

steps.text: Use appropriate methods and personal protective equipment (PPE).
Use a handsaw instead of an oscillating saw to prevent the generation of aerosols.
Avoid contact of the saw with brain tissue.
Apply ample coverage of a 10% bleach solution during and after the procedure for decontamination of exposed or contaminated surfaces and equipment.

preconditions.text: It is not possible to open the skull or remove the brain within a BSC, such as during an autopsy or routine diagnostics.

constraints.text: PPE must include dedicated laboratory clothing, heavy or chainmail gloves to avoid cuts or sticks, and an N95 respirator combined with a face shield or a PAPR to protect skin and mucous membranes.

Additional primary containment and personnel precautions, such as those described for BSL-3, are indicated for activities with a high potential for droplet or aerosol production, or involving large production quantities or high concentrations of infectious materials.

Personnel working in pressurized suits should be trained in the strategic use of penning, gating, and animal restraint equipment to minimize potential contact with animals, animal waste, and sharp surfaces.
A minimum of two workers should be present in the containment area at all times (i.e., a "buddy system") or other means of monitoring worker safety in containment.
All staff should be trained on appropriate response procedures for time-sensitive emergencies involving workers who are pinned or otherwise trapped by equipment or animals.

steps.text: Use the original level of containment for the intact pathogen.
Review inactivation procedures and methods to verify efficacy of inactivation procedures regularly (annually is recommended for high-risk agents, based on risk assessment for lower risk agents).
Review inactivation procedures and methods to verify efficacy.
Review inactivation procedures and methods to verify efficacy.

preconditions.text: The inactivation status of incoming materials is uncertain.

Conditions (e.g., higher volumes or concentrations of material, temperature, matrix material) have differed from the pre-determined inactivation procedure conditions set by the confirmation study(ies).
A previously verified inactivation procedure fails.

constraints.text: Communicate about any hazards that may be present in inactivated samples, risk assessments, sampling strategy, labeling, training, and retention of experimental data.
Thoroughly track inactivation and verification specifics for senders, internal recipients, transport personnel, and external recipients.

steps.text: Define design intent by developing an Owner’s Project Requirements (OPR) document.

Identify performance requirements from the perspective of stakeholders including the researchers, directors, technicians, operators, community, and any other parties that will be affected by the outcome of the laboratory design.
Carefully outline the stakeholders’ specific requirements for the proposed use of each space.
Differentiate between an actual requirement and a wish-list.
Define the requirements and base assumptions about the use of the laboratories and other spaces.
Include the hours and conditions when a space is likely to be occupied, partially occupied, or unoccupied.
Identify areas where worker schedules are most predictable.
Comment on the acceptable time-period for system start-ups during unanticipated or emergency use.
Differentiate between routine laboratory testing and uncommon activities (such as testing for high-consequence pathogens) during risk assessment.
Select PPE to provide the appropriate level of protection needed.
Provide laboratory professionals with information, resources, and training to build safety competencies.

constraints.text: Increasing the amount or use of PPE does not always indicate an increase in safety; PPE must not compromise the health of the laboratory professional or their ability to safely perform duties.

Training must address safety competencies including understanding hazards, knowledge of control measures (BSCs, PPE, safer sharps) and their limitations, ability to evaluate effectiveness, and commitment to safety.

steps.text: Conduct a site-specific comprehensive risk assessment to prioritize response procedures.
Involve relevant community members (e.g., first responders, infectious disease specialists) in the risk assessment and plan development.
Ensure a clinical laboratory emergency response plan exists based on the risk assessment.
Communicate the emergency response plan to staff.
Train staff on the plan to ensure they are capable of executing the specific procedures.
Post hazard communication in the form of signage and SOPs to assist staff during unexpected events.

constraints.text: Working in a clinical laboratory environment will always involve some level of risk, and unintended events, including incidents and accidents, will occur.
